opening.title: Every debate starts as a single claim.
opening.body: Two debaters and a moderator work on one shared map — not parallel monologues. Here's the opening position, visible to everyone.

decompose.title: Break a claim into the parts it rests on.
decompose.body: You can't agree or disagree with a slogan. The opening claim is pulled apart into the specific sub-claims that actually carry it.

consensus.title: Nothing lands until everyone agrees.
consensus.body: A link turns "agreed" only when both debaters and the moderator accept it. Structure on this map is earned, never just asserted.

counter.title: The other side builds on the same structure.
counter.body: The counter-argument doesn't open a new thread — it attaches exactly where it bears on the claim, so the disagreement stays in full view.

contradiction.title: Pinpoint where two claims actually collide.
contradiction.body: A rebuttal links one claim to the precise claim it contradicts. Both sides first agree on where they disagree — the step most arguments skip.

classification.title: The same words can be a different KIND of claim.
classification.body: Is this a value, a prediction, or a fact? Tagging each claim's kind exposes the most common way debates derail: treating one kind of statement as another.

axiom.title: Follow the "why" until it hits bedrock.
axiom.body: Some claims can't be argued further — they're where a worldview rests. The map marks these axioms instead of pretending the gap is about evidence.

interpretive_split.title: Sometimes the fight is over what a sentence even means.
interpretive_split.body: One claim is split into its distinct readings — one epistemic, one metaphysical — so each is argued on its own terms instead of past each other.

finale.title: A debate you can actually see.
finale.body: What's agreed, what's still contested, and the axioms underneath — a shared structure, not a transcript of two people talking past each other.

intro.text: Good evening, and welcome to a-conversa. Tonight's question: should zoos exist? Anna defends the affirmative; Ben, the negative. I don't take sides — I run the board. The goal isn't to win; it's to find out exactly where you actually disagree.

floor.text: Anna, the floor is yours — open with your core position, one sentence if you can. Ben, hold your response.

anna_opens.text: Modern accredited zoos should exist because, on balance, they do more good than harm — for conservation, for public understanding of wildlife, and for the animals in their care.

decompose_ask.text: That sentence is doing a lot of work. I'd like to decompose it: a definitional node up front, an umbrella claim, and three supporting legs. And is 'modern accredited zoos' a definitional move — are unaccredited operations simply out of scope?

anna_scope.text: The decomposition is faithful — those are exactly the three legs. And yes, it's definitional: I'm defending AZA-accredited institutions and their equivalents, not roadside menageries.

bridge_flag.text: Flagging for both of you: these claims are normative — at some point we'll need the bridge from the empirical claims to the 'should'. Ben, do you accept the scope and the map?

ben_scope.text: I accept it as the scope — but log that the accredited/unaccredited line does argumentative work: Anna gets to disown the worst cases. And I'll want a leg of my own later, on the intrinsic cost of captivity.

log_annotation.text: Logging Ben's acceptance with the annotation attached, so it doesn't get lost.

ben_withdraw.text: One more thing before I build: I'm suspending my agreement that the welfare leg supports the umbrella. If captivity carries a cost that doesn't fold into welfare, that support is exactly what's in question.

ben_captivity.text: Here's the leg: confinement of wide-ranging, cognitively complex animals imposes a morally significant cost on the individual — independent of welfare outcomes — and every benefit has to be netted against it.

operationalize.text: Ben, that's compound — splitting it into the cost claim and the netting claim. And operationalization on the cost claim: what evidence would change your mind? Is it defeasible, or do you want an axiom-mark?

ben_defeasible.text: Defeasible. If welfare science and revealed preference converged on no remaining unmet interest in well-managed captives, I'd drop it. The warrant is interest-in-agency: a contented prisoner is still wronged.

warrant.text: Capturing the defeater with its pre-committed rebut, and the warrant: the constitutive-capacities principle plus the empirical claim about what these animals' capacities are. Ben — is the principle freestanding for you?

ben_capabilities.text: Honestly, no — it's downstream of a capabilities view: a form of flourishing proper to a kind of creature. Push hard enough and you hit my actual floor: a life has a shape it is owed.

classify_normative.text: Capturing that floor as its own node — proposing it as normative.

ben_value_pushback.text: Dispute on the kind. It isn't a norm I'm arguing for — it's bedrock value. Classify it as what it is.

reclassify_value.text: Fair. Withdrawing 'normative'; proposing it as a value claim.

ben_axiom.text: And yes — propose the axiom-mark on it for me. That's my floor; anything I'd offer in support already presupposes it. The capabilities frame above it stays answerable to argument.

anna_axiom.text: I won't reject Ben's axiom — pretending lives don't have shapes to win a debate would be dishonest. Mark it for me too. But the reduction runs the other way: welfare is how we know which capacities count.

shared_axiom.text: Two axiom-marks on one node — shared bedrock from different frames. For the audience: the question is no longer whether lives have shapes that can be wronged. It's how we know which shapes, and what follows.

reword.text: One operationalization pass, Anna — you said 'almost always'. Tightening the wording to something the platform can work with: 'in nearly all cases'.

split.text: I heard two claims that need separate lines: how we identify constitutive capacities — epistemic — and whether they just are welfare in disguise — metaphysical. Proposing an interpretive split.

anna_reframe.text: Then let me name the reframe: if welfare is the access to constitutive capacities, the husbandry-netting question is the operational form of this dispute — not a sidestep of it.

anna_candidates.text: Candidates exist — the hatchery salmon's spawning run, wolf-pack structure, the bonsai we call stunted without invoking suffering. But each is contestable; I may be projecting a species-narrative.

ben_contests.text: I'm contesting the reframe — cleanly. The epistemic reading I accept outright, and I won't press the point Anna already conceded; that would be scoring points, not thinking. But the netting frame quietly demotes the axiom we both marked: owing comes first.

crux.text: So the live disagreement is sharp, small, and real: does owing come first, with welfare-accounting downstream — or do they enter together? That's the crux. Everything else was scaffolding to find it.

deadlock.text: Recording it as a live disagreement — not a failure; a located crux.

wrap.text: After the break we put the crux to them directly. Same table, same tool, narrower question. Stay with us.

hero.title: See exactly where a disagreement actually is.
hero.hypothesis: Most disagreements aren't really about the thing being argued. Either someone is quietly contradicting themselves, or both sides are treating the same statement as a different kind of claim — a fact, a value, a prediction — and talking past each other. a-conversa makes that structure visible, so the real disagreement has nowhere left to hide.

participants.title: Two debaters, one moderator
participants.body: Two people argue a single question while a moderator steers the structure, not the verdict. Everyone is working the same problem at the same time.

sharedGraph.title: One shared, living graph
sharedGraph.body: Every claim, objection, and connection lands on one map that grows as the debate does — not two separate transcripts. What each side is responding to stays in plain view.

consensus.title: Nothing lands until everyone agrees
consensus.body: A claim or a link only joins the agreed structure when both debaters and the moderator accept it. Structure is earned, never just asserted.

contradictions.title: Internal contradictions
contradictions.body: When two claims a side has accepted cannot both be true, the graph shows exactly where they collide — instead of letting the conflict stay buried.

categories.title: Category mismatches
categories.body: Is this a fact, a value, or a prediction? Tagging each claim's kind exposes the most common way debates derail: treating one kind of statement as another.

axioms.title: Bedrock axioms
axioms.body: Follow each "why" until it stops. Some claims cannot be argued further — they are where a worldview rests. The map marks these instead of pretending the gap is about evidence.

openSource.title: Built in the open
openSource.body: a-conversa is open source. Read the code, self-host your own instance, or adopt the debate format for your own community — the method matters more than the platform.

cta.title: Start a debate
cta.body: Spin up a session and put a real disagreement on the map — or sign in to pick up where you left off.

footer.tagline: a-conversa — structured debate you can see.

errors.whitespaceOnly: Display name cannot be only whitespace.
errors.tooLong: Display name must be at most 64 characters.
errors.invalidCharacter: Display name contains a disallowed character (control / bidi-override / non-printable).

errors.generic: Could not save your display name. Please try again.

supports.label: Supports
supports.description: Source provides evidence or backing for target.

qualifies.label: Qualifies
qualifies.description: Source hedges the scope or degree of target — "usually", "in most cases", "except when X".

bridges-from.label: Bridges from
bridges-from.description: Outgoing from a warrant node to the data it draws on.

bridges-to.label: Bridges to
bridges-to.description: Outgoing from a warrant node to the claim it licenses.

defines.label: Defines
defines.description: Source provides the meaning of a term used in target.

contradicts.label: Contradicts
contradicts.description: Source and target conflict; both cannot be true.

axiomMark.label: Axiom mark
axiomMark.pendingSrLabel: Pending axiom mark from participant {participantId} — not yet committed

prompt.question: What evidence would change your mind on this?
prompt.guidance: Empirical evidence routes to re-classification; a different value or principle routes to re-classification; truth-by-meaning routes to re-classification; 'nothing could change my mind' routes to axiom-mark; specific retraction conditions route to defeater capture.

prompt.question: What's the unstated bridge from "{nodeWording}" to your conclusion?
prompt.questionGeneric: What's the unstated bridge from the target to your conclusion?
prompt.guidance: The articulated bridge becomes a new node with bridges-from and bridges-to edges connecting the data and the claim — often the actual disagreement once surfaced.

capturePane.placeholder: Type the retraction condition that defeats this statement…
capturePane.ariaLabel: Defeater wording — what would defeat the selected statement?

metaMoveKindSelector.ariaLabel: Meta-move kind — pick reframe / scope-change / stance

metaMoveAction.ariaLabel: Propose a meta-move (reframe / scope-change / stance) about the selected target

reason.notConnected: the session is not connected — reconnecting…
reason.targetMissing: pick a target node or edge — click one to stage it
reason.targetKindInvalid: meta-moves target nodes or edges — clear the annotation target and pick a node or edge
reason.kindMissing: pick a meta-move kind (reframe / scope-change / stance)

rebutEdgePreCommit.hint: Pre-commit the retraction: agreeing here records that if this condition holds, it would defeat the target.

proposalFilter.stateChipLabel: {state, select, all {All} ready {Ready to commit} disputed {Disputed} other {Unknown}}

idle.label: Idle
idle.description: Waiting for the moderator's next move.

capture-statement.label: Capture statement
capture-statement.description: Compose a new statement and classify it before proposing.

decompose.label: Decompose
decompose.description: Break the selected statement into its component claims.

interpretive-split.label: Interpretive split
interpretive-split.description: Surface multiple readings of the selected statement when the wording admits more than one.

capture-defeater.label: Capture defeater
capture-defeater.description: Record a rebuttal against the selected statement.

operationalization.label: Operationalization
operationalization.description: Make a disputed statement testable by naming its conditions.

warrant-elicitation.label: Warrant elicitation
warrant-elicitation.description: Surface the warrant that licenses the inference from data to claim.

meta-move.label: Meta-move
meta-move.description: Capture a reframe, scope change, or stance about the discussion itself.

axiom-mark.label: Axiom-mark
axiom-mark.description: Mark a statement as a bedrock position the participant will not retreat from.

isOughtPrompt.question: Does this disputed wording carry an ought-claim that needs to be separated from what is?
isOughtPrompt.guidance: If yes, route toward decomposition of the normative claim. If no, continue by eliciting the warrant that links data to the claim.

action.decompose: Suggested next: decompose the normative claim

chooser.headerEdge: Choose a supports edge to break

synthetic.heading: Synthetic sessions
synthetic.intro: Generate a synthetic session to look at — no live participants required.
synthetic.generateError: Couldn't generate the session. Try again.

empty.title: Empty session
empty.description: A bare session: created, with the moderator and two debaters joined. No structure.

structured.title: Structured session
structured.description: A small worked log: a captured statement, a classification proposal, agreeing votes, and a commit.

walkthrough.title: Walkthrough session
walkthrough.description: The full worked debate fixture: decomposition, edges, annotations, axiom-marks, an interpretive split, and a snapshot — a deep log for exercising the scrubber and inspectors.

snapshotList.empty: No snapshots in this session yet.
snapshotList.rowAriaLabel: Jump to snapshot {label} at position {position, number}

cycle.title: Cycle in supports
cycle.detail: These statements support each other in a closed loop, so nothing in the loop is grounded outside of itself. This is circular reasoning.
cycle.action: Break one supports edge, decompose a node in the cycle, or have a participant axiom-mark a node where the chain terminates.

contradiction.title: Contradiction
contradiction.description: {count, plural, one {# contradiction} other {# contradictions}} between agreed statements
contradiction.detail: An agreed contradicts edge holds between two statements whose substance is agreed. Both cannot be true at the same time, so forward progress is blocked until the participants engage with the conflict.
contradiction.action: Decompose one or both statements, amend one to remove the conflict, or accept the contradiction as a bedrock disagreement and have each side axiom-mark their position.

multi-warrant.title: Multiple warrants
multi-warrant.description: {count, plural, =2 {Two warrants bridge the same data to the same claim: {nodes}} other {# warrants bridge the same data to the same claim: {nodes}}}
multi-warrant.detail: Two or more warrants license the same data-to-claim inference along different bridges. Each warrant is anchoring on a different aspect of the claim, which is a strong signal that the claim is bundling multiple things.
multi-warrant.action: Consider decomposing the claim so each warrant attaches to the component it actually licenses. Advisory: no action is required if the participants do not see the bundling.

dangling-claim.title: Dangling claim
dangling-claim.description: Claim has no incoming supports, rebuts, or bridges-to: {nodes}
dangling-claim.detail: A statement is positioned as a claim (something being engaged with) but has no incoming justification. Not an error — claims can stand briefly before being supported — but a claim that remains dangling is either being implicitly accepted or implicitly conceded.
dangling-claim.action: Prompt the defender for support, or ask whether the claim is being conceded or accepted.

coherency-hint.title: Coherency hint
coherency-hint.description: {count, plural, one {# unusual configuration detected: {kind}} other {# unusual configurations detected: {kind}}}
coherency-hint.detail: The graph contains an unusual edge or node configuration — for example, an incomplete warrant (a bridges-from with no matching bridges-to, or the reverse) or a self-contradicts edge. The system never blocks on this; it is advisory only.
coherency-hint.action: Review the flagged configuration. Repair it if it was an accident, or leave it if the structure is intentional.
